data.title: China Automotive Systems: No Breakthrough Is No Reason To Walk Away
data.description:
China Automotive Systems Inc (CAAS), a supplier of power steering components and systems for the automotive industry, has been putting the bulls to the test by trying their patience. The stock has basically gone sideways for well over a year in the charts because it is hamstrung by what appears to be resistance, which has prevented CAAS from moving beyond a certain point, even though there were a number of instances when it appeared CAAS was going to break through and move higher. This inability to move higher beyond a certain point is likely to cause at least some to give up and move on to greener pastures, but there is nonetheless reason to be optimistic that CAAS has a more than decent shot at breaking through on a future attempt, especially since headwinds have recently receded in strength.

CAAS gets rejected again

A previous article from October 2025 written by myself mentioned how CAAS was facing a number of headwinds, which had not only grown in strength in prior months, but also increased in number. For instance, supply chain disruptions caused by internal problems at Nexperia, a leading supplier of automotive chips, had erupted shortly before, which became an issue for automakers because it led to a shortage of needed inputs, including at automakers sourcing components from CAAS. Yet, while the possibility existed CAAS would have to deal with some sort of adverse impact, CAAS was arguably in a position to handle these headwinds. I thus decided to rate CAAS a buy in the article.

The chart above shows how CAAS has tested the patience of the bulls. CAAS is actually off to a good start in 2026, but similar to what happened a number of times before, the stock had its way higher cut off because the rally only managed to get as high as $4.98 on January 12, before CAAS pulled back. This is reminiscent of what happened a number of times before, including in September 2025, March 2025 and as far back as October 2024.

In all these instances, as shown in the chart above, the stock struggled to go higher in or around the $4.80-5.20 region, brief spikes notwithstanding. The stock last closed at $4.63, which means CAAS has already appreciated by a respectable 8.7% this early into 2026. However, the move and the resulting gains do not mean that much since they are offset by the fact that the same resistance that has held CAAS back since October 2024, or well over a year, remains in place. CAAS can try again, but for now the road higher looks blocked off.

Why headwinds have lost strength recently

While CAAS has not been able to overcome the resistance imposed on it, there are some encouraging developments worth noting that likely contributed to the recent rise in the stock. They could also eventually allow for another and perhaps more successful attempt at breaking through, or at least improve the likelihood of it happening. For starters, some of the headwinds mentioned in the previous article from October have receded to a certain degree.

One headwind mentioned was the issue of automakers having problems sourcing automotive chips from Nexperia, which had the potential to slow or even halt production of automobiles. This would in turn affect all other suppliers, including CAAS. However, while the internal dispute at Nexperia remains unresolved, the automotive industry has found a workaround of sorts by buying wafers directly from Nexperia Europe and have them shipped to Nexperia China for further processing to get finished products. This process is not ideal, but it should in theory allow for production to proceed smoothly, provided all relevant parties stick to it.

Another headwind that has receded in strength is the issue of rare earth elements. While China’s export controls on rare earth remain in place, trade talks between the U.S.-China have resulted in increased shipments of rare earth elements to qualified importers. So it appears the likelihood of supply chain problems due to rare earth has receded, at least at this time.

Why there is reason to be bullish CAAS

It needs mentioning that both the Nexperia issue and the rare earth issue could potentially flare up again. Still, assuming both issues remain subdued, then that could help pave the way for the stock to move higher, especially in combination with other supporting factors acting as catalysts. It just so happens that CAAS has a number of attributes that are likely to convince potential buyers that CAAS deserves to trade at a higher valuation and is thus worth betting on. This could cause them to get in on CAAS, pushing the stock price higher.

For instance, the automotive industry has been shaken up by a number of headwinds in the past year or so. Nexperia and rare earth were mentioned earlier, but other issues like U.S. tariffs on finished products like autos and tariffs on key inputs needed like steel and aluminum have also given the automotive industry a headache.

However, it is encouraging to see how despite all the problems in the automotive industry. CAAS itself has seen minimal impact from it all. On the contrary, CAAS has been able to do rather well considering the less than ideal circumstances. In the most recent or Q3 FY2025 report, for instance, CAAS posted strong results after it grew revenue by 17.7% YoY to $193.2M, which resulted in adjusted EPS of $0.32, up from $0.18 one year ago.

In the first three quarter of FY2025, revenue grew by 16.1% YoY to $536.5M and adjusted EPS grew by 17.4% YoY to $0.81, even with all the various issues the automotive industry had to contend with during this time. In a further sign of how well CAAS is coping with the current environment, CAAS raised its outlook by forecasting FY2025 revenue to end up at $730M by the end of the year, which implies Q4 FY2025 revenue of $193.5M since revenue in Q1-Q3 FY2025 totaled $536.5M.

This would make FY2025 the fifth consecutive year of growth since FY2020, the last year of no growth, which can be attributed to the lockdowns caused by COVID-19. My estimate is that FY2025 EPS will likely end up at around $1.13, assuming Q4 FY2025 EPS stays flat QoQ at $0.32. This implies CAAS is trading at just 4.09x estimated FY2025 earnings with the stock last closing at $4.63.

It also implies EPS managed a CAGR of 17.87% in FY2022-2025, using FY2022 EPS of $0.69 as the base and eFY2025 EPS of $1.13. At the same time, it is prudent to leave room for the possibility CAAS might be affected by the headwinds the automotive industry is facing, which could affect earnings growth in the coming years and result in a somewhat lower growth rate.

If we keep projecting and assume CAAS is able to keep growing revenue and earnings at the demonstrated double-digit pace in the next three years or FY2025-2028, but lower than before at an estimated CAGR of 10% to account for the potential impact from industry headwinds, then this implies CAAS is available at a PEG of less than one, or 0.41x, with CAAS trading at a P/E of 4.09x, which the methodology espoused by Peter Lynch suggests makes CAAS undervalued relative to growth.

The same methodology points to a fair value much higher than the current stock price of $11.30, or $1.13 times 10, for CAAS, assuming a 10% CAGR for earnings, which takes into account what CAAS could conceivably achieve under present industry conditions and what it has historically demonstrated to be capable of.

CAAS also looks undervalued based on the balance sheet. According to the Q3 FY2025 report, CAAS has cash and cash equivalents totaling $139.42M, which is close to its market cap of $139.7M. CAAS is actually valued at less than half its book value of $12.56 and the stock trading at $4.63, which equals CAAS trading at a price-to-book of 0.37x. This also points to a fair value well above the current stock price, or more like in the low teens similar to the previous method. In short, both the income statement and the balance sheet are supportive of the case for long CAAS.

Structural shift in the automotive world favor CAAS as an automotive supplier

There is another reason as to why CAAS is likely to trade at a higher valuation than it does now. It’s true that CAAS is exposed to a number of automakers that are struggling for a variety of reasons, but this is something that CAAS can handle. For instance, Stellantis (STLA) is CAAS’ top customer, having contributed about a fifth of FY2024 revenue, and Stellantis has not only been affected by the headwinds mentioned earlier, like rare earth supplies, but it has also experienced pressure on its competitive position in the global auto market, including in sales.

On the other hand, roughly two-thirds of revenue at CAAS is derived from China, including from such automakers like BYD, a name that has risen in recent years. The significance of this is that automakers from China are gaining global market share, which should drive growth at CAAS. Chinese automakers, for instance, managed to rank number one in terms of vehicle sales in 2025. They surpassed Japanese automakers, which held the number one spot for two decades.

This growth should rub off on suppliers like CAAS. While sales to some customers like STLA might suffer, this should be offset by growth elsewhere, resulting in little to no overall impact. The recent earnings report are encouraging in that sense to those long CAAS because they are consistent with this dynamic taking place. CAAS, which is more exposed to the better-performing and stronger part of the automotive industry and less to the weaker part, has an advantage.

In comparison, not all suppliers in the automotive world are as fortunate as CAAS. For instance, while there are many suppliers, the leading supplier of power steering systems is Bosch. Bosch is much larger than CAAS and it supplies many more parts than just power steering to automakers all around the world, but Bosch is reportedly expected to miss its sales target for 2025.

This after Bosch already took various measures throughout 2025, including mass layoffs, to deal with the various problems facing not just the company, but the industry as a whole. In contrast, while CAAS faces many of the same problems Bosch and others are seeing, it has still managed to grow by double digits. This is a clear sign of strength at CAAS and something to be bullish about.

Is CAAS without risk?

While CAAS has managed to deal with unfavorable industry conditions, the risk is that there is a further deterioration in the automotive world to such an extent that it starts to adversely affect even CAAS. While headwinds have receded to a certain extent recently, they are still very much out there. This possibility is the biggest risk facing CAAS in the short term. Anyone long CAAS needs to be aware of this.

There is also the risk that CAAS as a U.S.-listed stock that primarily operates in China could get tangled up by increased tensions between the U.S. and China. Most of the shares are also held by a small number of insiders, primarily the founder of CAAS, which some may see as a risk because the company is effectively under their control.

Investor takeaways

Most of the leading automakers, whether in the U.S., Europe or Japan, have been affected by industry headwinds. Tariffs, for instance, has and continues to be an issue for automakers, but some like those in China have been less impacted because they do not much of a presence in the U.S. Yet automakers in China are not without their own problems.

The biggest one is arguably what the Chinese government has referred to as “involution”, which basically entails the Chinese auto market in a state of extreme competition. The market is characterized by persistent price cuts by competitors to retain market share, which makes it difficult to make profits.

Many Chinese automakers have been able to offset whatever issues they are facing at home by turning to export markets. This has allowed them to gain market share because they have been able to grow faster than the traditional stalwarts. CAAS, which mainly supplies to these automakers, is reaping the benefits of these structural and therefore long-term changes taking place in the automotive industry.

This dynamic provides a solid base for long CAAS because it provides CAAS with the catalyst that can allow it to keep growing both the top and the bottom line at the double-digit pace recorded in the past 3-5 years. As detailed earlier, CAAS arguably deserves to trade significantly higher than it does now, possibly into the low teens, based on projected earnings growth in the income statement and book value on the balance sheet.

So while there is no denying that CAAS has been essentially stuck for over a year as it has been unable to decisively get past $5.00 or so, there is also reason to remain optimistic that CAAS can get it done by breaking through the resistance standing in the way and moving higher. The fact that CAAS has not just shown resilience with so much going against it in the industry, but even thrive is very encouraging to see and an attribute worth having. I am therefore bullish on CAAS with the above in mind, even though CAAS has admittedly not appreciated by all that much in the past 18 months or so.

Bottom line, CAAS has yet to really deliver in term of returns. Yet there is reason to remain confident it can happen down the line. CAAS has a number of things going for it. While it’s true they have yet to translate into stock gains, it is worth sticking with long CAAS because there is a good chance it will eventually happen.

data.title: Intel: Weak Guidance Matters More Than Reasonable Earnings
data.description:
As Intel Corporation's (INTC) shareholder, I was amazed by the market's latest activity driving the stock price up by a lot. I saw Intel's stock price grow by nearly 50% just over the course of the last month. To be honest, I found it quite surprising - yes I knew the market was expecting a reasonable earnings report, but let's be honest - buying INTC at these prices was risky business.

The last time I covered Intel I declared it a Hold as its stock price hiked after many months of poor performance. I established my long position in May 2025 and as I explained in my last coverage, I trimmed. I did not sell the whole position as I wanted to participate in INTC's further story.

As I was watching its stock price nearing $50 per share just before a very important press release, I sold another stake at just below $50 per share. Don't get me wrong - I still own some shares that account for around 40% of my initial position.

I feel quite lucky with the decision I made as Intel's pre-market price indicates that the Mr. Market is not happy with the earnings release. Intel is likely to open at over 10% downside from yesterday's close, which I consider a sharp reaction. Still, I think it's justified given two factors:

  1. I believe the Q4 performance itself was fine, especially as Intel topped expectations by a bit. The issue lies with its modest guidance for the upcoming quarter.
  2. I don't think previous stock price growth was justified, making this sharp drop quite a natural reaction to disappointing guidance.

I will go through this step by step, starting with INTC's earnings review, but I want to make one thing clear. I still own ~40% of my initial position in Intel, despite trimming two times. I intend to hold onto this stake unless valuation or business change materially. Intel remains a Hold for me.

Intel had a reasonable Q4 2025 performance

I believe many investors have mixed feelings about INTC's results - both revenue and gross margins were down on year-over-year basis. Revenue dropped by 4.1% - I think some may take it as another bad sign, but I don't think that to be the case.

What's important to me is that INTC's quarterly revenue was much higher than that delivered in Q1-Q2 2025, which was also true for Q3 2025. Yes, there's also seasonality at play here, but I would be very concerned if Q4 2025 was closer to the performance of the first half of 2025. As far as gross margins go, of course I'd prefer to see higher rather than lower levels, but I'd like to highlight that 37.9% was sufficient to allow Intel to generate ~$1.2B of operating income.

Even Intel's annual revenue was down when compared to 2024. But the management has a reasoning behind it - let me paste it right below quoting from the Earnings Call:

For the full year, revenue was $52.9 billion, down slightly year-over-year due to constraints across our own manufacturing network and with external suppliers which limited growth, especially in the second half.

I think there's another positive note to these results - they were above the management's guidance shared last year. INTC has beaten its own expectations in terms of both revenue and gross profit:

Fourth quarter revenue was $13.7 billion at the high end of the range we provided in October.

(...)

Q4 marks the fifth consecutive quarter of revenue above our guidance even as we navigate industry-wide supply constraints for our key products. Non-GAAP gross margin came in at 37.9%, approximately 140 basis points ahead of guidance (...)

We delivered fourth quarter non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.15 versus our guidance of $0.08 and driven by higher revenue, stronger gross margins and continued spending discipline.

Here's what I see - revenue higher by $0.4B vs the outlook shared in October, higher gross margin (~140 bps), and nearly double the expected EPS. Given Intel's struggles over the last couple of years, I have to admit - I'm satisfied with Intel's Q4 2025 performance.

Unfortunately, the management shared quite a modest guidance

And here's where I found a lot of bad news. Intel's guidance for Q1 of 2026 is far from optimistic. We discussed three key metrics above in terms of Q4 2025 and I presented them in a relatively positive light, but this ends here.

EPS is expected to get back to $0 - down $0.13 at y/o/y basis and $0.15 at quarterly basis. Gross margin continues to shrink and I see the management expects it to reach 34.5% in Q1 2026. Will that be enough to ensure INTC's operating profitability? I doubt that, especially given a break-even level of expected EPS.

The range for revenue outlook is quite wide from where I'm standing - I see the management expects $11.7B-12.7B, which would be down by $0.5B at midpoint.

That's what I was afraid of and that concern was one of the main reasons I sold another stake of my shares. It's not like Intel can make a turn-around story at such a short time. It could happen, but it will take longer than the market expected (judging by recent dynamic stock price growth).

What's the reason behind these expectations? Here's the management's take on that - I'm pasting it below:

Now turning to guidance. During the second half of 2025, we supported strong demand for our products with intra-quarter wafer production and inventory on hand. As we enter 2026, our buffer inventory is depleted and the mix shift in wafers towards servers, which began in Q3 will not come out of fab until late Q1 '26. As a result, and as we stated last quarter, our internal supply constraints are most acute in Q1.

In light of these dynamics, we are forecasting a Q1 revenue range of $11.7 billion to $12.7 billion. The midpoint of $12.2 billion reflects a lower end of seasonal Q1.

My conclusion is simple - beware of dynamic stock price increases but don't sell in panic

As I said at the beginning - Intel is a Hold for me. I'm very suspicious of rapid stock price increases in turn-around cases such as Intel - I recommend you the same approach.

That's why I did not believe in the $50+ stock price. Not at this point, the business hasn't been restructured enough for such a high valuation to be sustainable. That's why I trimmed the second time.

I believe it could be the case at some point - that's why I'm going to hold onto some shares, but my position is much lower than it used to be originally. Intel has much more work to do, much more improvement to show in its financials -ideally I'd like to see such improvements sustainable and not one-off scenarios in any given quarter.

I agree with Wall Street Analysts who, on average, expect Intel's stock price to decrease by ~18%. I'm pasting the chart below for you to review.

The upside potential is limited for now, but I believe those who are patient will be able to benefit with Intel. At the same time, I don't recommend buying at these levels - investors should give Intel time to turn things around. Frankly, INTC remains a Hold for me.

data.title: MUB: Largest Investment-Grade Muni Bond ETF, But Not A Buy At These Levels
data.description:
It's been a bit less than a year since I've covered the iShares National Muni Bond ETF (MUB), the largest muni bond ETF in the market, and something of a stand-in for quality munis as an asset class. Last time I covered the fund I argued that t-bills and investment-grade corporate bonds offered higher after-tax income for the vast majority of investors, making MUB and most of the quality muni ETFs sub-par investments. Since then, rates for t-bills and most bonds are down, muni bond rates flat. Spreads have narrowed between munis and other types of bonds, although perhaps not by enough to justify an investment in MUB. I maintain my hold rating, but the situation could easily change in the next few months.

MUB and Munis - After-Tax Yields

MUB is an investment-grade muni bond index ETF. Due to its broad index and diversified portfolio, it is something of an industry benchmark and should perform in-line with quality munis as an asset class.

For these securities, the most important consideration is their after-tax yields. Insofar as these are good and higher-than-average, the investments merit a buy. If not, then a sell or a hold. As after-tax yields are strongly dependent on the specific circumstances of these investors one can't draw absolute conclusions for these securities, but some general trends do seem clear.

Right now, MUB yields 3.2%, slightly lower than most bonds and bond sub-asset classes. Standardized SEC yields are a bit higher, at 3.4%, but the same is true for most types of bonds. Figures for MUB and index ETFs tracking the major bond sub-asset classes are as follows:

Although MUB's dividend yield is somewhat below-average, after-tax yields seem a bit more competitive. As per my calculations, and using SEC yields, the breakeven tax rate for muni bonds versus bonds is 19.0%. Investors facing higher marginal tax rates than that would receive more in income from MUB than BND, and viceversa. Breakevens for muni bonds versus investment-grade corporate bonds are at 30.6%, appreciably higher. I'm not sure which of these rates is more material, or which comparison is fairest, but I lean towards the latter. Bonds in general include treasuries, tilting the scales in favor of MUB due to the quality / low-yield of these securities. Investment-grade corporate bonds are high-quality securities, but not treasuries, which seems a fairer comparison to MUB.

Another way of looking at after-tax yields is through taxable equivalent yields. These basically tell you how much in taxable income do you need to match the income of a tax-free investment, depending on the yield of said investment, and on the marginal tax rate. Table of these is as follows, be sure to focus on the 3.5% tax-free column, closest figure to MUB's 3.4% SEC yield.

In my opinion, the conclusion of the above is quite simple: muni bonds offer higher after-tax yields to investors in the higher tax brackets, not so much for those in the lower brackets. This is generally true, but not always, and there is always value in the confirmation, and in the specific figures here.

On the other hand, muni bonds don't seem to offer materially higher after-tax income than some of my favorite quality ETFs. The best example by far is the Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF (BOXX), a synthetic t-bills options ETF. Returns have been extremely similar to those of t-bills since inception, as should be the case moving forward:

BOXX's strategy aims for zero distributions, although there was a special distribution last year. The lack of distributions means no taxable events, which should allow investors to defer any taxes until they sell their position, presumably at an opportune time. Gains should be taxed at capital gain rates, which are generally lower than income tax rates. BOXX's strategy is more dependent on management execution and, let's say regulatory forbearance, than most. It could stop working in the future, but it has worked quite well in the past, minus that one distribution.

BOXX's returns should, and have, closely track t-bills, which currently yield 3.62%:

That is marginally higher than MUB's 3.4% SEC yield, with BOXX offering a much more stable share price. Comparing their returns since inception shows this quite clearly:

Spreads should flip after another cut, which should occur in the next few months. Even then, MUB would only yield a marginal amount more than BOXX.

As should be clear from the above, MUB offers comparable after-tax income to BOXX, at much greater risk and volatility. Right now, BOXX seems like the broadly superior investment, although could things as rates continue to go down.

Besides BOXX, there are lots of quality income ETFs with yields in the 5.0% - 6.0% range, most of which should offer higher after-tax yields than MUB. As an example, the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY) focuses on quality MBS and ABS, it yields 5.6%, excluding a large distribution in late 2024, with a 5.5% SEC yield. That should mean higher after-tax income for most investors, marginally less for those in the highest tax bracket. CARY's share price and returns are much more stable too, as are total returns since inception.

As there are lots of differences between MUB, CARY, and BOXX, and because after-tax yields are somewhat dependent on the specific circumstances of each investor, perhaps MUB is the better choice for some investors. In my opinion, BOXX and CARY seem broadly superior, and should be of greater interest to more investors.

MUB - Looking Back

I wrote a broadly similar article to this one on MUB last December. Since then, MUB has significantly underperformed most bonds and bond sub-asset classes on a before-tax basis. Underperformance is significant enough that after-tax returns are almost certain to be lower too.

MUB's underperformance was caused by comparatively weak capital gains, with most bonds seeing single-digit gains, MUB close to zero.

The flipside of the above is that before-tax spreads have narrowed, increasing the relative attractiveness of MUB as an investment. The impact here is too small to warrant a change in rating, however.

Besides the above, t-bill rates have declined by 0.75%, decreasing the attractiveness of these investments, including BOXX. Said ETF still seems stronger than MUB overall, but by much less than before. Although this doesn't warrant a change in rating either, the impact is much more significant, and rates could continue to fall.

Conclusion

MUB is the largest muni bond ETF in the market. Although there is nothing significantly wrong with the fund, several of its peers offer competitive after-tax yields. As such, I would not be investing in the fund at the present time.

data.title: Alibaba: Brace For Dip After Exceptional Price Performance YTD (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Since I last checked on the Chinese e-commerce giant Alibaba (BABA) in September 2025, it's price has barely moved, as it corrected in the last two months of the year after rising until the end of October 2025. 2026, though, has started on a strong note, with the stock up by 21% YTD already.

The rise is impressive enough on its own, but it's even more so when seen in context. Here I first discuss what sets the rise apart, followed by two arguments for why the price bullishness might not last, as well as a discussion on the company's recent growth drivers and BABA's valuations.

BABA outdoes both the sector and peers

BABA's rise is compared with three sets of tickers here. The first is the key market indices relevant to it, followed by how it compares to both AI related and consumer discretionary sector related ETFs. And finally, a look at compared with peers as taken. The details are as follows.

  • Market indices: The S&P 500 (SP500) has barely moved so far, up by just under 1% YTD. Chinese indices have done better, with the Han Seng (HSI) and Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP) up by around 4% (see Chart 1 below). Still, these increases are nothing compared with BABA.
  • Sector performance: Next, let's look at the sectors in which it operates. First, AI, and tech, more generally. The iShares A.I. Innovation and Tech Active ETF (BAI) and Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AIQ) are up by just 4% and 3% respectively. Even the consumer discretionary ETF, the State Street Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLY) has seen just 2.7% increase.
  • Comparison with peers: A similar case is visible when comparing BABA with its direct competitors, the Temu owner PDD Holdings (PDD) and JD.com (JD). PDD is actually down by almost 7% YTD, and JD has seen around 5% increase (see Chart 2 below).

Why caution is called for

It's still early days in the year. But the comparisons raise the question - what's driving BABA? Two key developments, as it turns out. They make for good reasons for the stock's rise, to be sure. But they come with their own challenges, indicating that the euphoria around BABA might not last. These are as follows.

#1. Chips and security

After news that Nvidia’s H200 AI chips could now be permitted for import into China, BABA rose by 3% in intraday trading on January 9. While the stock still ended the trading session with down by 2%, it gained over 10% on the following Monday.

There were good reasons for the uptick. A Reuters report states that "Chinese companies are keen on the H200 as its ability to train AI models is currently unmatched by domestic equivalents...". Alibaba was one of the companies that wanted the chips imported into the country, along with TikTok owner ByteDance (BDNCE), following Trump approving the same in December 2025 followed by a formal green light in mid-January.

However, it hasn't been an easy road forward since. For one, Beijing decided to restrict the import of chips after all. While the Chinese government hasn't provided reasons for its decision, it could well be a way to stimulate domestic production considering that the economy just hasn't gone back to its pre-pandemic high growth levels.

Moreover, the US isn't entirely happy about exporting to China either. Despite Trump's approval, the AI Overwatch Act is now advancing, which, if approved would allow at least a stalling if not outright block to these exports. Moreover, news that Alibaba could be listed as among China's military companies from last November suggests that there can further risk trade with the company going forward.

#2. Chip unit IPO planned, or is it?

Another recent reason for the uptick was the news yesterday that Alibaba could take its chip unit T-Head public. The stock was up by 5% at yesterday's close.

Alibaba doubled down on chip design last year amid challenges in importing Nividia's chips. The effort paid off as it entered into a deal with the country's second biggest wireless services provider Unicom. Alibaba doesn't release T-Head's financials separately, but this deal indicates demand potential for domestically developed chips as well as its ability to provide them.

Here's the catch, though. While the unit is so far a part of the parent company, the IPO would entail spinning it off into a separate one. For this reason, I'd take the news with a pinch of salt going by the company's past. Consider the following:

  • In 2024, the company pulled out of the IPO of its logistics division, Cainiao Smart Logistics Network, to synchronise it better with the company's e-commerce division, and there could have been other reasons as well.
  • In 2023, Alibaba had intended to take the cloud intelligence division public as well. A decision that was withdrawn following challenges with the import of US manufactured chips.
  • Then there's the case of the payment platform Ant Financial, which was spun off from Alibaba in 2011. Ant was meant to get publicly listed in 2020, but the IPO was cancelled.

The point here is, that so far, Alibaba's history with spin-offs being successfully listed isn't encouraging at all. As a result, too much stress on T-Head's potential IPO can be hasty.

Cloud intelligence races ahead

That said, even if Nvidia's chips aren't successfully imported into China and T-Head doesn't go for an IPO, it's clear that Alibaba is already racing ahead in the AI race. In its Q2 FY26 (quarter ending September 2025, financial year ending March 2026) results, the company cites consulting firm Omdia's report on China's AI cloud market for H1 2025, which shows Alibaba is the biggest player in the segment with a market share of ~36%.

Robust AI growth is also reflected in the segment wise results for Q2 FY26. The cloud intelligence division saw a strong increase of 34% YoY during the quarter and the company points out that "AI-related product revenue achieving triple-digit year-over-year growth for the ninth consecutive quarter". The segment's contribution to revenue also grew to 16.1% from 13.5% in Q1 FY26.

The segment's EBITA also grew by a robust 35% YoY. Interestingly, its contribution to the EBITA jumped to ~40% from 7.6% in Q1 FY26 as the overall EBITA fell by 78% YoY due to a sharp contraction on the e-commerce front. Alibaba has invested heavily in e-commerce aspects like quick commerce and technology, which explains the overall EBITA drop.

Market multiples are stretched

In my previous article on BABA, I had arrived at a fair valuation of the stock based on both its past levels and keeping in mind the rising significance of AI to the company. The valuation was based on the weighted average of one, the forward P/Es of the four biggest holdings in the AIQ, with BABA being the fifth and two, of BABA's historical valuation.

The fair valuation has dropped substantially to 15.1x now compared to 25.8x in September 2025, as Oracle (ORCL) and Tesla (TSLA) have now been replaced by Micron Technology (MU) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) among AIQ's top holdings, which have substantially lower forward P/Es. BABA's rise in the meantime, has resulted in a jump in its forward P/E to ~28x compared with 20.9x then. Even the forward P/Es for the next two years don't subside back to its fair valuation.

What next?

The forward P/E right now implies that BABA is trading at almost double the levels it should ideally be at. Admittedly, there are a number of variables to consider in the fair valuation. Not the least of which is the valuations of the biggest holdings in AIQ. But even then, the fact remains that there are AI related companies in the market trading at far lower valuations than BABA.

It doesn't help that Alibaba faces risks related to the US. Not only are its chip imports repeatedly getting stalled, if it's listed as a Chinese military company, trading with the US can become even harder. The stock market joy over its own chip unit T-Head's potential IPO is also premature going by the company's past experiences with spin offs.

Even then, the company has shown its mettle in recent years with rapid growth in cloud intelligence and AI related services. And if all goes as it is now, they will become only more dominant growth drivers for Alibaba going forward. It's just that keeping the risks in mind along with BABA's stretched valuations, further price rise, even if it happens, might not sustain it in the foreseeable future. I'm downgrading BABA to Hold.

data.title: Intel: Buy Into The Pullback
data.description:
Introduction

Intel Corporation (INTC) has been one of the most polarizing companies available on the market as I’ve rarely seen a firm benefiting from such tailwinds in such a short time, as highlighted by the Nvidia (NVDA) investment. With the company releasing its latest earnings, I want to see if it is still worthy of a Buy rating, as it has appreciated nearly 42% since I last reviewed it in October.

Current Dynamics

There is a lot to decipher from the company’s recent earnings, and I’ll start with the basics. Intel posted a Q4 non-GAAP of 15 cents, a beat of $0.07 based on revenues of $13.7B, a beat of $310MM. Now you might be wondering why the company is dropping like a stone pre-opening, and that might have been the result of its lackluster forecast and manufacturing problems.

The Q1 projections came well below what Wall Street was estimating, as apparently “Chipzilla” is still struggling with supply chain bottlenecks and a shifting market landscape. Meaning that the production issues have continued to hamper the comeback attempt by Intel, which has heavily disappointed investors who were anticipating a boost from new products. Right now, it is suffering from low yields, which made it harder to fill orders, which is disappointing considering that INTC has spent years trying to restore its technological edge and recover from the loss of its dominant market share. The available supply will hit its lowest point in Q1, pointing to a difficult period ahead, especially with the shortage of memory chips.

Diving into the segments, Data Center & AI revenue increased by 9% Y/Y to $4.7B, which is the bright spot as demand for server CPUs alongside AI accelerators has surged, but Intel mentioned that it couldn’t keep up with the volume, which is both a huge tailwind as well as a disappointment in terms of manufacturing. Client Computing saw revenue decrease to $8.2B as the hype around AI PCs was not enough to offset slower sales, though Intel remains optimistic about the rollout of its new Core Ultra Series 3 under Panther Lake architecture. And finally, Intel Foundry, which has been the core of the pivot for the company, is highly important for the current pivot. Revenue was up 4% Y/Y to $4.5B as it is underway on the 18A process now in high-volume manufacturing in Arizona and Oregon.

Nvidia and Intel also closed their transaction, adding a $5B liquidity boost, helping Intel to fund its ambitious factory expansion. CEO Lip-Bu Tan also emphasized that Intel is weeks/months ahead of TSMC (TSM) in the high-volume production of 2nm-equivalent chips (18A), which is the key for the company to regain market leadership.

Overall, despite the barrage of negatives, I still believe that this was a good quarter for Intel, as it proved in a way that it can still manufacture world-class chips and secured a massive partnership with Nvidia. They have officially exited the emergency phase and entered the growth phase of their plans. They simply don’t have enough factory capacity to turn that into profit yet. Plus, the problem is that based on current financials, Intel has a much larger room to fall if it continues to disappoint shareholders.

Valuation

Due to the current turnaround, general metrics like P/E won’t tell us the whole story about Intel, as analysts are currently pricing the stock on the potential 2027-2028 recovery. By the time the 18A factors are fully ramped, earnings are expected to jump; on a 2028 forward basis, Intel is trading at a fairly reasonable 23x earnings, though it requires investors to wait two years for the payoff. There is a case that Intel’s business should be valued as two different businesses, Foundry and Products. On a bull case, the Foundry could be valued as a national champion akin to TSMC, with my estimate of the foundry assets being worth north of $150B. But right now, Intel is a story stock as the current valuation is not supported by cash flows but by all the deals and its prime position in chip manufacturing.

Looking ahead, I’m particularly enthusiastic about the fact that the company is now technically ahead of TSMC in high-volume production of 2nm-equivalent chips. Right now, Panther Lake is using backside power delivery, which is making it much more efficient than current laptop chips. Clearwater Forest, which is the server equivalent of the 18A node, aimed at taking back the data center market share that AMD’s EPYC chips have been chipping away at. Intel is also betting that geopolitical tensions will force American companies to shift production to U.S. soil. Intel is currently courting mega clients for its 14A node, with key decisions supposed to come in H2 2026, giving us a lot of clarity about the trajectory ahead. Lip-Bu Tan also set the bold target of 45% market share in the AI PC segment, with more than 200 AI-enabled laptop designs launching during the year.

Intel currently has a margin problem, that’s for sure. Current gross margin is standing at 37.9% following the recent quarter, which is fairly low for a leading-edge semiconductor company. That’s mainly due to the company paying double as they are spending billions to build new factories while still paying competitors to build some of the company’s current chips, such as Lunar Lake, until they are ready to manufacture everything themselves. Foundry is currently a massive money pit as it reports an operating loss of around $2.5B per quarter, and that’s why Lip-Bu Tan implemented a welcome fiscal discipline regime, with divestments and OpEx reduction keeping the company from bleeding out during the 18A ramp-up. The company is still targeting 60% gross margins and 40% operating margins by 2030. But to get to all of that, Intel needs to bag a mega-whale foundry customer and hit its manufacturing yields target.

Risks

The biggest risk would come from execution as the company has proven that yes, it can design 1.8nm chips, but can it mass-produce them profitably? The yields are in line with internal targets, but they are vastly below the industry standard needed for high profitability. If the yields do not improve rapidly, the company will continue to lose money on every wafer it produces. Any further delay for the 18A ramp and the upcoming 14A node would cause a massive exodus of investors who have priced in a flawless execution.

There is also the current cash burn problem, as the company is roughly spending $16-$18B a year on massive factories in Ohio and Germany, for example. The company’s FCF has been deeply negative for several quarters. And though it has stabilized its balance sheet, there is now very little room for error.

Conclusion

I still believe that Intel is worth investing in, considering that the company has done what many thought was impossible by catching up to TSMC. They are still lagging behind profitability and production-wise, but that is why the company has received so much interest in it, it’s a long-term play that requires patient investors and in the end pays off. I keep my buy rating on Intel.

data.title: December Readers ID'd 9 Ideal 'Safer' Dividends In 40 Dogs
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May, 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail or comment to the author has been fair game for a REader FAvorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, It is possible that only ROgues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Most valuable for the writer, however, are reader comments that catch errors in calculations or changes in direction, and figures to communicate. Examples include the reader who missed my "safer" dividend follow-up articles because they contain dividend payout ratios.

However the main subject of these articles are your stock ticker comments whether they be about favorites or rogues. Be sure to mention a ticker when you comment

The “safer” lists are now available on the regularly published side of Seeking Alpha but that has added to confusion, since my IDEAL stocks come in two flavors: (1) not so safe and (2) safe, which is preferable since the dividends are paid from cash flow, not borrowed money.

YCharts, says: “At this time we do not have a filter or screener criterion to identify stocks that explicitly pay "qualified dividends" per IRS standards. However, I am happy to pass along your feedback to our team if this is something that can be added on. I can't guarantee any changes, but they use feedback like that to help determine what enhancements should be added in future updates.”

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M

Your comments count and encourage better writing by the dogcatcher.

An observation from April 2023:

“Mr. Arnold, I enjoy reading your many articles and get some great ideas from them. However I find the number of charts are a bit of overkill, many duplicating the info. I'm sure you have your set format and reasons why, but I'd be happy with only two charts; your sort by target gains and your sort by div yield. It would make it a much easier read. Thank You —pmbrandt 15Apr2023”

I’d gladly just submit those two charts and ditch all the verbiage. However, fifteen years ago when I started submitting articles, the SA editorial team said they needed text to go with the graphics and topsy was born.

Still, there are frequent complaints of too many words for little information. In May somebody thought I was paid by the word. No such luck

In May. 2025 a reader, named Move on, commented on my Bloomberg Focus List article, “Death by data…just get to the point already…

My reply: @Move on Good point! Here's a hint: Forget the text. Read the charts and graphs. Those are the dots to connect

In January 2025 I got in trouble with readers when I neglected to define an acronym used to describe the Ben Graham Value collection. Unintroduced, I dropped a GASV acronym on them which stood for “Graham All-Star Value” stocks, dogs, or pack.

No doubt, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, Aristocrats, Fortune 500 Industry Leaders, MoPays, and all my other articles, less obfuscating.

In February, 2022 one reader suggested an option strategy for monthly-paying dividend stocks:

"You should identify where options are available on the Monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk."

[See a summary of ten fair-priced December ReFa/Ro Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and sixteen “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

More than one writer has decried my favoritism for low-priced stocks. They especially dislike my "ideal" stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equal or exceed share price.

A prime example is Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI); the satellite radio and pandora music catalog owner, spun off from Liberty media (and now listed as one of the S&P500).

A reverse-split re-priced SIRI in October 2024. Recently (1/22/26) priced at $20.66, SIRI still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 5.26%! A little under $1k investment now buys 48.4 shares. and they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) over 2.3 times the share price. SIRI, however, is in the doghouse this month because of negative return for the year of -2.92%.

Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 20 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services can survive that long)!

In December 2023, a reader suggested my share price equal or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10k invested in NVIDIA Corp (NVDA) could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly nobody buys NVDA for the dividend, just growth. (On 1/9/26 $1.00 of NVDA dividend was priced at $4,587.16)

However, I was reminded of a comment in May, 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. To clarify: if the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3.00%, the dividend paid from $1.000.00 invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

February 17, 2025 reader wdjaxOn responded to my suggestion that besides waiting for the market prices to drop or higher dividends to be declared for stocks, that stock splits could also make dividends from $1k invested exceed the single share price of the stock.

Stay tuned. I now include stock splits in my suggestions to those who determine the price of dividends.

Here is a useful reader report from CEFreeman.com as of May, 2025:

“Interesting! Not only have I discovered I am an accidental Income Investor, but evidently I am also a Dog Catcher. If I am understanding this correctly. I don't have $1000 to invest each time, but I strive to buy enough of something so the dividends always buy 1 share or more. Usually, I try to buy 100 shares. Sure, the positions I buy are very low priced, but I must be doing something right. I am growing my income solidly, penny by penny! Dog Catching Income Investor. I like it.”

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 25.77% To 52.74% Net Gains From Top-Ten ReFa/Ro Dogs By December, 2026.

Five of ten top ReFa/Ro Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this December, 2025 yield-based forecast for ReFa/Ro dogs, as graded by Wall St. Wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 34.55% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ReFa/Ro stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 62% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield 24.14% Vs. (35) 23.28% Net Gains by All Ten, Come December, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten ReFa/Ro Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 3.71% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The five lowest-priced ReFa/Ro top-yield dogs for December were: Eagle point Credit; Orchid Island; Seven Hills Realty, Franklin BSP Realty and AGNC Investment Corp with prices ranging from $5.74 to $11.93.

Five higher-priced ReFa/Ro top-yield dogs as of December were: Ellington Financial; Westlake Chemical; TORM Plc; Alliance Resource Partners; NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF with prices ranging from $14.01 to $54.36.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the Holiday Seasonal euphoria in the markets dies and after Santa failed to rally, the Wall Street moguls slack work, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements gain momentum into 2026 with the exception of very few hot meme stocks, look for most of the ReFa/Ro Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly, with an assist from trade war clouds, or a pandemic of one sort or another.

Afterword

Buy Any of 9 IDEAL from 16‘Safer' December ReFa/Ro Dogs
